title: Interquartile range (IQR)
description: Practice finding the interquartile range (IQR) of a data set.
topic_path: Topic B: Lesson 7: Measuring variability for skewed distributions (interquartile range)

assessment_items.question_content:
The following data points represent the number of quesadillas each person at Toby's Tacos ate.

*Answer $2$ questions about the data set.*

1. **Sort the data from least to greatest.**

[[☃ sorter 1]]

2. **Find the interquartile range (IQR) of the data set.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] quesadillas
hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

Make sure to sort the numbers before finding the median, $Q_1$, and $Q_3$!

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by sorting the data points from least to greatest.  

$\underbrace{0\text{, }0\text{, }\dfrac{1}{4}\text{, }\dfrac{1}{2}\text{, }\dfrac{1}{2}}_\text{lower half}\text{, }  \purpleD{1}\text{, }\underbrace{1\text{, }1\text{, }\dfrac{5}{4}\text{, }2\text{, }2}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points. So the median is the middle number.

The median is $\purpleD{1}$.

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{0\text{, }0\text{, }\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}\text{, }\dfrac{1}{2}\text{, }\dfrac{1}{2}}_\text{lower half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{1} = \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{1\text{, }1\text{, }\tealE{\dfrac{5}{4}}\text{, }2\text{, }2}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{3} = \tealE{\dfrac54}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\
&=  \tealE{\dfrac{5}{4}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}} \\\\
&= \dfrac{4}{4} \\\\
&= 1 \end{align}$

The data from least to greatest is:

$0$, $0$, $\dfrac14$, $\dfrac12$, $\dfrac12$, $1$, $1$, $1$, $\dfrac54$, $2$, $2$

*Did you remember to sort the numbers?*

The interquartile range is $1$ quesadilla.

answers.message: Your answer is almost correct, but not quite. Remember not to include the median when calculating the first and third quartiles.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following data points represent the number of animal crackers in each kid's lunch box.

*Answer $2$ questions about the data set.*

1. **Sort the data from least to greatest.**

[[☃ sorter 1]]

2. **Find the interquartile range (IQR) of the data set.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] animal crackers
hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

Make sure to sort the numbers before finding the median, $Q_1$, and $Q_3$!

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by sorting the data points from least to greatest.  

$\underbrace{4\text{, }4\text{, }6\text{, }7}_\text{lower half}\text{, }\purpleD{10}\text{, }\underbrace{11\text{, }12\text{, }14\text{, }15}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points. So the median is the middle number.

The median is $\purpleD{10}$.

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{4\text{, }\blueE{4}\text{, }\blueE{6}\text{, }7}_\text{lower half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{1} = \dfrac{\blueE{4} + \blueE{6}}{2} = \dfrac{10}{2}= \maroonD{5}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{11\text{, }\goldE{12}\text{, }\goldE{14}\text{, }15}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{3} = \dfrac{\goldE{12} + \goldE{14}}{2} = \dfrac{26}{2}= \tealE{13}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\
&=  \tealE{13} - \maroonD{5} \\\\
&= 8 \end{align}$

The data from least to greatest is:

$4$, $4$, $6$, $7$, $10$, $11$, $12$, $14$, $15$

*Did you remember to sort the numbers?*

The interquartile range is $8$ animal crackers.

answers.message: Your answer is almost correct, but not quite. Remember not to include the median when calculating the first and third quartiles.

assessment_items.question_content:
A family of bears is going to a movie. The following data points represent the ticket and candy price (in dollars) for each bear.

*Answer $2$ questions about the data set.*

1. **Sort the data from least to greatest.**

[[☃ sorter 1]]

2. **Find the interquartile range (IQR) of the data set.**

  [[☃ numeric-input 1]] dollars
hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

Make sure to sort the numbers before finding the median, $Q_1$, and $Q_3$!

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by sorting the data points from least to greatest.  

$\underbrace{0\text{, }1\dfrac12\text{, }2\dfrac12\text{, }3}_\text{lower half}\text{, }\purpleD{4}\text{, }\underbrace{4\text{, }4\text{, }7\text{, }7\dfrac12}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points. So the median is the middle number.

The median is $\purpleD{4}$.

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{0\text{, }\blueE{1\dfrac12}\text{, }\blueE{2\dfrac12}\text{, }3}_\text{lower half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{1} = \dfrac{\blueE{1 \dfrac12} + \blueE{2 \dfrac12}}{2} = \dfrac{4}{2}= \maroonD{2}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{4\text{, }\goldE{4}\text{, }\goldE{7}\text{, }7\dfrac12}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{3} = \dfrac{\goldE{4} + \goldE{7}}{2} = \dfrac{11}{2}= \tealE{5.5}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\
&=  \tealE{5.5} - \maroonD{2} \\\\
&= 3.5 \end{align}$

The data from least to greatest is:

$0$, $1 \dfrac12$, $2 \dfrac12$, $3$, $4$, $4$, $4$, $7$, $7 \dfrac12$

*Did you remember to sort the numbers?*

The interquartile range is $3.5$ dollars.

answers.message: Your answer is almost correct, but not quite. Remember not to include the median when calculating the first and third quartiles.

A family of bears is going to a movie. The following data points represent the ticket and candy price (in dollars) for each bear.

*Answer $2$ questions about the data set.*

1. **Sort the data from least to greatest.**

2. **Find the interquartile range (IQR) of the data set.**

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

Make sure to sort the numbers before finding the median, $Q_1$, and $Q_3$!

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by sorting the data points from least to greatest.  

$\underbrace{0\text{, }1.50\text{, }2.50\text{, }3}_\text{lower half}\text{, }\purpleD{4}\text{, }\underbrace{4\text{, }4\text{, }7\text{, }7.50}_\text{upper half}$

The median is $\purpleD{4}$.

$\underbrace{0\text{, }\blueE{1.50}\text{, }\blueE{2.50}\text{, }3}_\text{lower half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{1} = \dfrac{\blueE{1.50} + \blueE{2.50}}{2} = \dfrac{4}{2}= \maroonD{2}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{4\text{, }\goldE{4}\text{, }\goldE{7}\text{, }7.50}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{3} = \dfrac{\goldE{4} + \goldE{7}}{2} = \dfrac{11}{2}= \tealE{5.5}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\

The data from least to greatest is:

$0$, $1.50$, $2.50$, $3$, $4$, $4$, $4$, $7$, $7.50$

*Did you remember to sort the numbers?*

The interquartile range is $3.5$ dollars.

answers.message: Your answer is almost correct, but not quite. Remember not to include the median when calculating the first and third quartiles.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following data points represent the number of remote controllers each student in Tria's video game club owns.

*Answer $2$ questions about the data set.*

1. **Sort the data from least to greatest.**

[[☃ sorter 1]]

2. **Find the interquartile range (IQR) of the data set.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] remote controllers
hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

Make sure to sort the numbers before finding the median, $Q_1$, and $Q_3$!

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by sorting the data points from least to greatest.  

$\underbrace{0\text{, }0\text{, }0\text{, }1\text{, }2}_\text{lower half}\text{, }\purpleD{2}\text{, }\underbrace{4\text{, }5\text{, }7\text{, }8\text{, }10}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points. So the median is the middle number.

The median is $\purpleD{2}$.

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{0\text{, }0\text{, }\maroonD{0}\text{, }1\text{, }2}_\text{lower half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{1} = \maroonD{0}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{4\text{, }5\text{, }\tealE{7}\text{, }8\text{, }10}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{3} = \tealE{7}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\
&=  \tealE{7} - \maroonD{0} \\\\
&= 7 \end{align}$

The data from least to greatest is:

$0$, $0$, $0$, $1$, $2$, $2$, $4$, $5$, $7$, $8$, $10$

*Did you remember to sort the numbers?*

The interquartile range is $7$ remote controllers.

answers.message: Your answer is almost correct, but not quite. Remember not to include the median when calculating the first and third quartiles.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following data points represent the number of classes that each teacher at Broxin High School teaches.

*Answer $2$ questions about the data set.*

1. **Sort the data from least to greatest.**

[[☃ sorter 1]]

2. **Find the interquartile range (IQR) of the data set.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] classes
hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

Make sure to sort the numbers before finding the median, $Q_1$, and $Q_3$!

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by sorting the data points from least to greatest.  

$\underbrace{1\text{, }1\text{, }3\text{, }4\text{, }4}_\text{lower half}\text{, }\purpleD{5}\text{, }\underbrace{5\text{, }5\text{, }6\text{, }7\text{, }9}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points. So the median is the middle number.

The median is $\purpleD{5}$.

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{1\text{, }1\text{, }\maroonD{3}\text{, }4\text{, }4}_\text{lower half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{1} = \maroonD{3}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{5\text{, }5\text{, }\tealE{6}\text{, }7\text{, }9}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{3} = \tealE{6}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\
&=  \tealE{6} - \maroonD{3} \\\\
&= 3 \end{align}$

The data from least to greatest is:

$1$, $1$, $3$, $4$, $4$, $5$, $5$, $5$, $6$, $7$, $9$

*Did you remember to sort the numbers?*

The interquartile range is $3$ classes.

answers.message: Your answer is almost correct, but not quite. Remember not to include the median when calculating the first and third quartiles.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following data points represent the number of points scored by each player on the Wildcats basketball team last game.

*Answer $2$ questions about the data set.*

1. **Sort the data from least to greatest.**

[[☃ sorter 1]]

2. **Find the interquartile range (IQR) of the data set.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] points
hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

Make sure to sort the numbers before finding the median, $Q_1$, and $Q_3$!

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by sorting the data points from least to greatest.  

$\underbrace{4\text{, }5\text{, }6\text{, }\redE{8}}_\text{lower half} \text{, } \underbrace{\redE{9}\text{, }10\text{, }11\text{, }13}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points. So the median is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$\dfrac{\redE{8} + \redE{9}}{2} = \purpleD{8.5}$ points

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles, but we *do* include the numbers we averaged to get it.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{4\text{, }\blueE{5}\text{, }\blueE{6}\text{, }{8}}_\text{lower half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{1} = \dfrac{\blueE{5} + \blueE{6}}{2} = \dfrac{11}{2}= \maroonD{5.5}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{{9}\text{, }\goldE{10}\text{, }\goldE{11}\text{, }13}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{3} = \dfrac{\goldE{10} + \goldE{11}}{2} = \dfrac{21}{2}= \tealE{10.5}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\
&=  \tealE{10.5} - \maroonD{5.5} \\\\
&= 5 \end{align}$

The data from least to greatest is:

$4$, $5$, $6$, $8$, $9$, $10$, $11$, $13$

*Did you remember to sort the numbers?*

The interquartile range is $5$ points.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following data points represent the number of attendees at each of the dance events hosted by Cloy Events.

*Answer $2$ questions about the data set.*

1. **Sort the data from least to greatest.**

[[☃ sorter 1]]

2. **Find the interquartile range (IQR) of the data set.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] attendees
hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

Make sure to sort the numbers before finding the median, $Q_1$, and $Q_3$!

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by sorting the data points from least to greatest.  

$\underbrace{32\text{, }34\text{, }35\text{, }\redE{35}}_\text{lower half}\text{, }\underbrace{\redE{39}\text{, }40\text{, }44\text{, }47}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points. So the median is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$\dfrac{\redE{35} + \redE{39}}{2} = \dfrac{74}{2}= \purpleD{37} $ attendees

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles, but we *do* include the numbers we averaged to get it.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{32\text{, }\blueE{34}\text{, }\blueE{35}\text{, }{35}}_\text{lower half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{1} = \dfrac{\blueE{34} + \blueE{35}}{2} = \dfrac{69}{2}= \maroonD{34.5}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{{39}\text{, }\goldE{40}\text{, }\goldE{44}\text{, }47}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{3} = \dfrac{\goldE{40} + \goldE{44}}{2} = \dfrac{84}{2}= \tealE{42}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\
&=  \tealE{42} - \maroonD{34.5} \\\\
&= 7.5 \end{align}$

The data from least to greatest is:

$32$, $34$, $35$, $35$, $39$, $40$, $44$, $47$

*Did you remember to sort the numbers?*

The interquartile range is $7.5$ attendees.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following data points represent the volume of gas in each race car driver's tank (in liters).

*Answer $2$ questions about the data set.*

1. **Sort the data from least to greatest.**

[[☃ sorter 1]]

2. **Find the interquartile range (IQR) of the data set.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{L}$
hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

Make sure to sort the numbers before finding the median, $Q_1$, and $Q_3$!

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by sorting the data points from least to greatest.  

$\underbrace{2.8\text{, }4\text{, }4.3\text{, }6\text{, }\redE{7.5}}_\text{lower half}$, $\underbrace{\redE{8.5}\text{, }9\text{, }11.6\text{, }12\text{, }12.1}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points. So the median is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$\dfrac{\redE{7.5} + \redE{8.5}}{2} =\dfrac{16}{2}= \purpleD{8}$

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles, but we *do* include the numbers we averaged to get it.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{2.8\text{, }4\text{, }\maroonD{4.3}\text{, }6\text{, }{7.5}}_\text{lower half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{1} = \maroonD{4.3}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{{8.5}\text{, }9\text{, }\tealE{11.6}\text{, }12\text{, }12.1}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{3} = \tealE{11.6}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\
&=  \tealE{11.6} - \maroonD{4.3} \\\\
&= 7.3 \end{align}$

The data from least to greatest is:

$2.80$, $4$, $4.3$, $6$, $7.5$, $8.5$, $9$, $11.6$, $12$, $12.10$

*Did you remember to sort the numbers?*

The interquartile range is $7.3\,\text{L}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following data points represent the number of hours each of Daniel's friends slept on the night before the first day of school.

*Answer $2$ questions about the data set.*

1. **Sort the data from least to greatest.**

[[☃ sorter 1]]

2. **Find the interquartile range (IQR) of the data set.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] hours
hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

Make sure to sort the numbers before finding the median, $Q_1$, and $Q_3$!

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by sorting the data points from least to greatest.  

$\underbrace{2.6\text{, }3\text{, }4.9\text{, }5\text{, }\redE{5}}_\text{lower half}\text{, }\underbrace{\redE{6}\text{, }6\text{, }7.9\text{, }8\text{, }8.2}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points. So the median is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$\dfrac{\redE{5} + \redE{6}}{2} = \dfrac{11}{2}= \purpleD{5.5} $

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles, but we *do* include the numbers we averaged to get it.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{2.6\text{, }3\text{, }\maroonD{4.9}\text{, }5\text{, }{5}}_\text{lower half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{1} = \maroonD{4.9}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{{6}\text{, }6\text{, }\tealE{7.9}\text{, }8\text{, }8.2}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{3} = \tealE{7.9}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\
&=  \tealE{7.9} - \maroonD{4.9} \\\\
&= 3 \end{align}$

The data from least to greatest is:

$2.6$, $3$, $4.9$, $5$, $5$, $6$, $6$, $7.9$, $8$, $8.2$

*Did you remember to sort the numbers?*

The interquartile range is $3$ hours.

assessment_items.question_content:
Bev's Bingo Hall hosts bingo night every week. Players try to win by being the first ones to complete their bingo sheet in the given pattern.

Each dot in the following dot plot represents the number of players at one table in Bev's bingo hall.

[[☃ image 2]]

**Find the interquartile range (IQR)  of the data in the dot plot.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] players
hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by listing the data points from least to greatest.  

$\underbrace{0\text{, }1\text{, }1\text{, }3}_\text{lower half}\text{, }\purpleD{3}\text{, }\underbrace{3\text{, }4\text{, }5\text{, }7}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points. So the median is the middle number.

The median is $\purpleD{3}$.

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{0\text{, }\blueE{1}\text{, }\blueE{1}\text{, }3}_\text{lower half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{1} = \dfrac{\blueE{1} + \blueE{1}}{2} = \dfrac{2}{2}= \maroonD{1}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{3\text{, }\goldE{4}\text{, }\goldE{5}\text{, }7}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{3} = \dfrac{\goldE{4} + \goldE{5}}{2} = \dfrac{9}{2}= \tealE{4.5}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\
&=  \tealE{4.5} - \maroonD{1} \\\\
&= 3.5 \end{align}$
The interquartile range is $3.5$ players.

options.alt: A dot plot has a horizontal axis labeled Number of players marked by 8 equidistant tick marks numbered from 0 to 7. Dots are plotted above the following: 0, 1; 1, 2; 3, 3; 4, 1; 5, 1; 7, 1.

answers.message: Your answer is almost correct, but not quite. Remember not to include the median when calculating the first and third quartiles.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lacrosse is a field sport where teams try to score the highest number of points by scoring goals against their opponents' net.

Each dot in the following dot plot represents the number of goals Luis scored in one lacrosse season.

[[☃ image 2]]

**Find the interquartile range (IQR)  of the data in the dot plot.**

 [[☃ numeric-input 1]] goals
hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by listing the data points from least to greatest.  

$\underbrace{43\text{, }44\text{, }44\text{, }44}_\text{lower half}\text{, }\purpleD{{45}}\text{, }\underbrace{45\text{, }47\text{, }48\text{, }48}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points. So the median is the middle number.

The median is $\purpleD{45}$.

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{43\text{, }\blueE{44}\text{, }\blueE{44}\text{, }44}_\text{lower half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{1} = \dfrac{\blueE{44} + \blueE{44}}{2}= \dfrac{88}{2} = \maroonD{44}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{45\text{, }\goldE{47}\text{, }\goldE{48}\text{, }48}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{3} = \dfrac{\goldE{47} + \goldE{48}}{2} = \dfrac{95}{2}= \tealE{47.5}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\
&=  \tealE{47.5} - \maroonD{44} \\\\
&= 3.5 \end{align}$
The interquartile range is $3.5$ goals.

options.alt: A dot plot has a horizontal axis labeled Number of goals numbered from 43 to 48. Dots are plotted above the following: 43, 1; 44, 3; 45, 1; 47, 1; 48, 2.

answers.message: Your answer is almost correct, but not quite. Remember not to include the median when calculating the first and third quartiles.

assessment_items.question_content:
Each dot in the following dot plot represents the number of chocolate chips in one cookie in a package.

[[☃ image 2]]

**Find the interquartile range (IQR)  of the data in the dot plot.**

 [[☃ numeric-input 1]] chocolate chips
hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by listing the data points from least to greatest.  

$\underbrace{2\text{, }3\text{, }3\text{, }4\text{, }4}_\text{lower half}\text{, }\purpleD{4}\text{, }\underbrace{4\text{, }5\text{, }5\text{, }6\text{, }7}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points. So the median is the middle number.

The median is $\purpleD{4}$.

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{2\text{, }3\text{, }\maroonD{3}\text{, }4\text{, }4}_\text{lower half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{1} = \maroonD{3}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{4\text{, }5\text{, }\tealE{5}\text{, }6\text{, }7}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{3} = \tealE{5}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\
&=  \tealE{5} - \maroonD{3} \\\\

The interquartile range is $2$ chocolate chips.

hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by listing the data points from least to greatest.  

$\underbrace{0\text{, }0\text{, }0\text{, }1\text{, }2}_\text{lower half}\text{, }\purpleD{2}\text{, }\underbrace{2\text{, }2\text{, }2\text{, }3\text{, }4}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points. So the median is the middle number.

The median is $\purpleD{2}$.

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{0\text{, }0\text{, }\maroonD{0}\text{, }1\text{, }2}_\text{lower half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{1} = \maroonD{0}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

assessment_items.question_content:
Each dot in the following dot plot represents the height of one toddler at Mrs. Orlando's daycare.

[[☃ image 2]]

**Find the interquartile range (IQR)  of the data in the dot plot.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by listing the data points from least to greatest.  

$\underbrace{81\text{, }82\text{, }83\text{, }\redE{83}}_\text{lower half} \text{, }\underbrace{\redE{84}\text{, }84\text{, }84\text{ ,}85}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points. So the median is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$\dfrac{\redE{83} + \redE{84}}{2} = \dfrac{167}{2}= \purpleD{83.5}$

The median is $\purpleD{83.5}$.

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles, but we *do* include the numbers we averaged to get it.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{81\text{, }\blueE{82}\text{, }\blueE{83}\text{, }83}_\text{lower half} $

There was an *even* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{1} = \dfrac{\blueE{82} + \blueE{83}}{2} = \dfrac{165}{2}= \maroonD{82.5}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{84\text{, }\goldE{84}\text{, }\goldE{84}\text{ ,}85}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{3} = \dfrac{\goldE{84} + \goldE{84}}{2} = \dfrac{168}{2}= \tealE{84}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\
&=  \tealE{84} - \maroonD{82.5} \\\\
&= 1.5 \end{align}$
The interquartile range is $1.5\,\text{cm}$.

options.alt:
"A dot plot.  The number line, labeled Height (centimeters), is numbered 80 to 86. 
The tick mark 81 has 1 dot.
The tick mark 82 has 1 dot.
The tick mark 83 has 2 dots. 
The tick mark 84 has 3 dots.
The tick mark 85 has 1 dot."

answers.message: We need to include the $83$ and $84$ in their respective halves when we calculate the quartiles.
This is the range, not the interquartile range.

assessment_items.question_content:
Each dot in the following dot plot represents the essay score of one student in Mr. Harrisburg's class.

[[☃ image 2]]

**Find the interquartile range (IQR)  of the data in the dot plot.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] points
hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by listing the data points from least to greatest.
  
$\underbrace{1\text{, }2\text{, }3\text{, }\redE{3}}_\text{lower half} \text{, } \underbrace{\redE{4}\text{, }4\text{, }4\text{, }6}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points. So the median is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$\dfrac{\redE{3} + \redE{4}}{2} = \purpleD{3.5}$

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles, but we *do* include the numbers we averaged to get it.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{1\text{, }\blueE{2}\text{, }\blueE{3}\text{, }{3}}_\text{lower half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{1} = \dfrac{\blueE{2} + \blueE{3}}{2} = \dfrac{5}{2}= \maroonD{2.5}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{{4}\text{, }\goldE{4}\text{, }\goldE{4}\text{, }6}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$Q_{3} = \dfrac{\goldE{4} + \goldE{4}}{2} = \dfrac{8}{2}= \tealE{4}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\
&=  \tealE{4} - \maroonD{2.5} \\\\
&= 1.5 \end{align}$
The interquartile range is $1.5$ points.

options.alt: A dot plot has a horizontal axis labeled Score on a 6-point scale numbered from 1 to 6. Dots are plotted above the following: 1, 1; 2, 1; 3, 2; 4, 3; 6, 1.

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] cavities
hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by listing the data points from least to greatest.

$\underbrace{0\text{, }0\text{, }0\text{, }1\text{, }\redE{1}}_\text{lower half} \text{, } \underbrace{\redE{3}\text{, }3\text{, }4\text{, }7\text{, }7}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points. So the median is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$\dfrac{\redE{1} + \redE{3}}{2} = \purpleD2$

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles, but we *do* include the numbers we averaged to get it.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{0\text{, }0\text{, }\maroonD{0}\text{, }1\text{, }{1}}_\text{lower half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{1} = \maroonD{0}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{{3}\text{, }3\text{, }\tealE{4}\text{, }7\text{, }7}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{3} = \tealE{4}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\
&=  \tealE{4} - \maroonD{0} \\\\
&= 4 \end{align}$
The interquartile range is $4$ cavities.

assessment_items.question_content:
Each dot in the following dot plot represents the number of songs on one of Shane's albums.

[[☃ image 2]]

**Find the interquartile range (IQR)  of the data in the dot plot.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] songs
hints:
The **interquartile range** is the amount of spread in the middle $50\%$ of the data set.

It is the positive difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

$\text{IQR} = Q_3-Q_1$

To find the quartiles, let's first find the median by listing the data points from least to greatest.

$\underbrace{7\text{, }9\text{, }9\text{, }10\text{, }\redE{{10}}}_\text{lower half} \text{, }\underbrace{\redE{{10}}\text{, }11\text{, }12\text{, }12\text{, }14}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *even* number of data points. So the median is the mean of the two middle numbers.

$\dfrac{\redE{10} + \redE{10}}{2} = \purpleD{10}$

We *do not* include the median when we calculate the quartiles, but we *do* include the numbers we averaged to get it.

The **first quartile** is the median of the data points  to the *left* of the median.

$\underbrace{7\text{, }9\text{, }\maroonD{9}\text{, }10\text{, }{{10}}}_\text{lower half} $

There was an *odd* number of data points in the lower half. So the first quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{1} = \maroonD{9}$

The **third quartile** is the median of the data points to the *right* of the median.

$\underbrace{{{10}}\text{, }11\text{, }\tealE{12}\text{, }12\text{, }14}_\text{upper half}$

There was an *odd* number of data points in the upper half. So the third quartile is the middle number.

$Q_{3} = \tealE{12}$

$\begin{align} \text{IQR} &= Q_3-Q_1  \\\\
&=  \tealE{12} - \maroonD{9} \\\\
&= 3 \end{align}$
The interquartile range is $3$ songs.
